items.question: What is the kernel trick in one sentence?
items.answer: Replace dot products ⟨φ(x), φ(x′)⟩ with K(x, x′) so algorithms like SVM run in an implicit nonlinear feature space without computing φ(x) explicitly.

items.question: Write the RBF (Gaussian) kernel formula and explain γ.
items.answer: K(x, x′) = exp(−γ ‖x − x′‖²). γ controls how fast similarity decays with distance: large γ means only very close points are similar; small γ yields smoother, more global boundaries.

items.question: What is the polynomial kernel and what do d, γ, and r mean?
items.answer: K(x, x′) = (γ x · x′ + r)^d. d is the polynomial degree (interaction order), γ scales the dot product, and r is a constant offset (coef0 in sklearn).

items.question: Why must the Gram matrix be positive semi-definite?
items.answer: PSD Gram matrices correspond to valid inner products in some feature space (Mercer's condition). Indefinite K breaks the geometric interpretation and can make kernel SVM optimization ill-posed.

items.question: When would you choose a linear kernel over RBF for an SVM?
items.answer: When features are already high-dimensional and informative (e.g. sparse TF-IDF text) and validation shows linear separation is sufficient — linear is faster and less prone to overfitting on small n.

items.question: How do kernels relate to deep learning representations?
items.answer: Kernels fix a similarity function K analytically; deep nets learn φ from data. Both lift inputs into a space where a simple decision rule (hyperplane or linear head) works — kernels are the classical hand-crafted path.
